edges.description: Failed LonP1 degradation of phosphorylated E1-alpha increases the inhibitory phosphorylated E1-alpha pool, lowering PDH activity and pyruvate oxidation.

edges.target: Cerebral energy failure and neurodevelopmental injury

edges.description: Impaired pyruvate oxidation increases lactate in blood and CSF.
Blocked PDH flux leaves pyruvate insufficiently cleared.
Lactate accumulation appears clinically as increased circulating lactate concentration.
Pyruvate accumulation appears clinically as increased circulating pyruvate concentration.

Cerebral energy failure and neurodevelopmental injury

edges.description: Energy failure in brain development is associated with structural brain malformations, including corpus-callosum dysgenesis.
edges.source: Cerebral energy failure and neurodevelopmental injury

edges.description: Structural brain abnormalities in PDH complex deficiency include ventriculomegaly.
edges.source: Cerebral energy failure and neurodevelopmental injury

edges.description: Chronic cerebral energy failure contributes to progressive neurological degeneration.

edges.source: Cerebral energy failure and neurodevelopmental injury

edges.description: Congenital lactic acidosis is the clinical manifestation of lactate accumulation.
Tachypnea can reflect respiratory compensation during metabolic acidosis.
Dyspnea is recorded in the PDH deficiency phenotype spectrum and can accompany severe metabolic decompensation.
Metabolic decompensation from lactic acidosis contributes to lethargy.
Feeding difficulty is part of the early severe presentation; the immediate intermediate is not resolved in this graph.
Vomiting is recorded in the E3 deficiency metabolic-decompensation phenotype spectrum.
Hypoglycemia is recorded in E3 deficiency and grouped here with metabolic decompensation features.
Hypotonia is one of the commonest signs in the PDC deficiency spectrum.
Global developmental delay is the clinical phenotype captured by this neurodevelopmental injury node.
PDH deficiency can resemble nonprogressive cerebral palsy, especially in female PDHA1 deficiency.
Growth delay is part of the broader developmental phenotype spectrum.
Intrauterine growth restriction is part of the broader developmental phenotype spectrum.
Seizures are a core manifestation of PDH-related cerebral energy failure.
Milder PDH deficiency states can include ataxia.
Spasticity is a pyramidal motor manifestation within the PDH neurologic phenotype spectrum.
Dysarthria is grouped with PDH-related movement and motor-control dysfunction.
Choreoathetosis is grouped with PDH-related movement disorder manifestations.
Gait disturbance follows from ataxic, dystonic, or pyramidal motor involvement.
Dystonia is directly supported as a PDH deficiency manifestation.
Tremor is grouped with the movement-disorder manifestations of PDH deficiency.
Pyramidal signs reflect motor pathway involvement in the PDH neurologic phenotype.
Abnormal eye movements are grouped with the neurologic movement-control phenotype.
Paroxysmal dystonia is a subtype-specific dystonic manifestation.
Broad-based gait is a subtype-specific gait manifestation linked to movement-system involvement.
Lower-limb hyperreflexia is grouped with pyramidal motor involvement.
Atypical behavior is grouped with neurologic involvement, with intermediate neurobehavioral mechanisms not resolved here.
Retinal degeneration is an E2-deficiency neurologic sensory phenotype.
Neurodegeneration is recorded as a frequent E2-deficiency manifestation.
Paroxysmal dystonia is recorded as a frequent E2-deficiency movement phenotype.
Broad-based gait is recorded as a frequent E2-deficiency gait phenotype.
Lower-limb hyperreflexia is recorded as a frequent E2-deficiency pyramidal sign.
This globus-pallidus imaging sign is recorded in E2 deficiency.
edges.target: Eye of the tiger anomaly of globus pallidus

edges.description: Atypical behavior is recorded in E2 deficiency, with intermediate neurobehavioral mechanisms not resolved here.
Low vitamin B1 is recorded in the E2 deficiency phenotype table; its mechanistic role is not resolved here.
Hepatic failure is an E3-deficiency systemic manifestation of DLD-related disease.

evidence.snippet: Manifestations range from often fatal, severe, neonatal lactic acidosis to later-onset neurological disorders.

nodes.description: The DLAT/E2 subtype is characterized by childhood lactic acidosis and neurological dysfunction, with Orphanet phenotype rows documenting retinal degeneration, neurodegeneration, paroxysmal dystonia, broad-based gait, lower-limb hyperreflexia, globus-pallidus imaging abnormalities, atypical behavior, and low circulating vitamin B1.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase E2 deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: A very rare form of pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency (PDHD) characterized by variable lactic acidosis and neurological dysfunction, mainly appearing during childhood.

evidence.snippet: Pyruvate dehydrogenase E3 deficiency is a very rare subtype of pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency (PDHD) characterized by either early-onset lactic acidosis and delayed development, later-onset neurological dysfunction or liver disease.

nodes.description: Dichloroacetate is a targeted pharmacologic approach intended to increase PDH complex catalytic activity and stability. Human trial evidence supports lactate lowering but not broad neurologic or clinical outcome improvement.

evidence.reference_title: Controlled clinical trial of dichloroacetate for treatment of congenital lactic acidosis in children.
evidence.snippet: DCA significantly decreased the rise in blood lactate caused by carbohydrate feeding.

evidence.reference_title: Controlled clinical trial of dichloroacetate for treatment of congenital lactic acidosis in children.
evidence.snippet: However, it did not improve neurologic or other measures of clinical outcome.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of long-term treatment of children with congenital lactic acidosis with dichloroacetate.
evidence.snippet: Although continued dichloroacetate exposure is associated with evidence of peripheral neuropathy, it cannot be determined whether this is attributable mainly to the drug or to progression of underlying disease.

evidence.reference_title: Phase 3 Trial of Dichloroacetate in Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex Deficiency:
evidence.snippet: The objective of this research study is to conduct a pivotal phase 3 trial of treatment with the investigational drug dichloroacetate (DCA) in young children with deficiency of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC).

nodes.description: Ketogenic diet is the best-supported disease-directed management strategy, bypassing the PDH block by providing ketone-derived acetyl-CoA and improving epilepsy, ataxia, sleep, language, social function, and hospitalization frequency in many patients.

evidence.reference_title: Ketogenic diet in pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency: short- and long-term outcomes.
evidence.snippet: The treatment had a positive effect mainly in the areas of epilepsy, ataxia, sleep disturbance, speech/language development, social functioning, and frequency of hospitalizations.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: The use of the ketogenic diet bypasses the metabolic block, by providing a direct source of acetyl-CoA, leading to amelioration of some symptoms.

meta.association: Causal germline pathogenic variant in the E1-alpha subtype record

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase E1-alpha deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: LONP1 | lon peptidase 1, mitochondrial | hgnc:9479 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations of LONP1 encoding the mitochondrial LonP1 protease cause pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency and profound neurodegeneration with progressive cerebellar atrophy.
evidence.snippet: We identified a novel homozygous missense LONP1 variant, c.2282 C > T, (p.Pro761Leu), by whole-exome and Sanger sequencing in two siblings born to healthy consanguineous parents.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic LONP1 variants can impair mitochondrial LonP1 protease degradation of phosphorylated PDH E1-alpha. Accumulation of inhibitory phospho-E1-alpha reduces PDH activity and produces a PDH deficiency biochemical phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations of LONP1 encoding the mitochondrial LonP1 protease cause pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency and profound neurodegeneration with progressive cerebellar atrophy.
evidence.snippet: We demonstrated that in the siblings' fibroblasts, PDH dysfunction was caused by increased levels of the phosphorylated E1α subunit of PDH, which inhibits enzyme activity.

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations of LONP1 encoding the mitochondrial LonP1 protease cause pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency and profound neurodegeneration with progressive cerebellar atrophy.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, in vitro studies demonstrated that purified LonP1-P761L failed to degrade phosphorylated E1α, in contrast to wild-type LonP1.

evidence.reference_title: The spectrum of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency: clinical, biochemical and genetic features in 371 patients.
evidence.snippet: Patients who died were younger, presented clinically earlier and had higher blood lactate levels and lower residual enzyme activities than subjects who were still alive at the time of reporting.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated blood or CSF lactate reports the lactate arm of pyruvate-to-lactate accumulation downstream of impaired PDH flux.

nodes.description: When pyruvate cannot efficiently enter the PDH complex reaction, pyruvate and lactate accumulate in blood, CSF, and tissues, producing the classic biochemical pattern of lactic acidosis with a lactate:pyruvate ratio often not exceeding 20.

evidence.reference_title: The spectrum of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency: clinical, biochemical and genetic features in 371 patients.
evidence.snippet: Although the clinical spectrum of PDC deficiency is broad, the dominant clinical phenotype includes presentation during the first year of life; neurological and neuromuscular degeneration; structural lesions revealed by neuroimaging; lactic acidosis and a blood lactate:pyruvate ratio ≤ 20.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylbutyrate therapy for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency and lactic acidosis.
evidence.snippet: A deficiency of any enzyme in these pathways results in inadequate removal of pyruvate and lactate from blood and tissues and causes lactic acidosis.

nodes.description: Lactic acidosis is a core biochemical and clinical manifestation of PDH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: The spectrum of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency: clinical, biochemical and genetic features in 371 patients.
evidence.snippet: lactic acidosis and a blood lactate:pyruvate ratio ≤ 20

evidence.reference_title: Manifestations of X-linked pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency in female PDHA1 carriers.
evidence.snippet: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency is in up to 90% caused by pathogenic variants in the X-linked PDHA1 gene.

nodes.description: Many PDHA1 E1-alpha pathogenic variants impair folding, E1 incorporation, or assembly of the PDH complex, lowering overall PDC activity.
nodes.id: PDHA1 variant folding and assembly defects

evidence.reference_title: Folding and assembly defects of pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency-related variants in the E1α subunit of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex.
evidence.snippet: Our studies suggest that pathogenic E1α variants may be associated with structural changes of PDC and impaired folding of E1α.

nodes.description: Residual PDH complex activity is modulated by phosphorylation of E1-alpha by pyruvate dehydrogenase kinases and dephosphorylation by PDH phosphatases, making kinase inhibition a plausible treatment target in selected variants.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylbutyrate therapy for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency and lactic acidosis.
evidence.snippet: Phosphorylation of specific serine residues of the E1α subunit of PDHC by pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase (PDK) inactivates the enzyme, whereas dephosphorylation restores PDHC activity.

evidence.snippet: A deficiency of any enzyme in these pathways results in inadequate removal of pyruvate and lactate from blood and tissues and causes lactic acidosis.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated pyruvate reports impaired clearance of the glycolytic end product when oxidative decarboxylation by PDH is blocked.

nodes.description: PDH complex deficiency blocks oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, weakening the link between glycolysis and the TCA cycle.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHc) is a mitochondrial matrix multienzyme complex that provides the link between glycolysis and the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle by catalyzing the conversion of pyruvate into acetyl-CoA.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylbutyrate therapy for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency and lactic acidosis.
evidence.snippet: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHC), an enzyme complex that catalyzes the irreversible conversion of pyruvate into acetyl-CoA (coenzyme A), plays a central role in linking glycolysis to the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and lipogenic pathways

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase E2 deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)

evidence.snippet: HP:0001250 | Seizure | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Severe and milder PDH deficiency states can produce seizures, ataxia, dystonia, and other movement disorders through energy failure, structural brain anomalies, and neurotransmitter disturbance.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency and epilepsy.
evidence.snippet: Milder deficiency states present with variable manifestations that include cognitive delay, ataxia, and seizures.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency presenting as isolated paroxysmal exercise induced dystonia successfully reversed with thiamine supplementation.
evidence.snippet: Dystonia precipitated by exercise may be the only symptom of a PDH deficiency

nodes.description: Sodium phenylbutyrate is an investigational pharmacologic approach that can inhibit PDK-mediated E1-alpha phosphorylation, increase PDH complex activity in responsive cells and models, and is under clinical study for PDH complex deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylbutyrate therapy for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency and lactic acidosis.
evidence.snippet: Phenylbutyrate increases PDHC activity in fibroblasts from PDHC-deficient patients harboring various molecular defects

evidence.reference_title: Comparison Between Dichloroacetate and Phenylbutyrate Treatment for Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Phenylbutyrate can only be used on patients with certain pathogenic variants (p.P221L, p.R234G, p.G249R, p.R349C, p.R349H) on the PDH protein.

evidence.reference_title: Pilot Clinical Trial to Investigate the Safety and Efficacy of Phenylbutyrate Therapy for Patients With Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: In this study phenylbutyrate is used for patients with pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001257 | Spasticity | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.snippet: HP:0002789 | Tachypnea | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: High-dose thiamine may benefit selected thiamine-responsive PDHA1 variants, particularly milder movement-disorder presentations.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency presenting as isolated paroxysmal exercise induced dystonia successfully reversed with thiamine supplementation.
evidence.snippet: Dystonia completely remitted after high doses of thiamine, remaining free of symptoms after 3 years of follow up.

nodes.description: Selected PDHA1 variants can retain thiamine-responsive residual activity, producing milder movement-disorder presentations that may improve with high-dose thiamine rather than representing broad responsiveness of all PDH complex subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency presenting as isolated paroxysmal exercise induced dystonia successfully reversed with thiamine supplementation. Case report and mini-review.
evidence.snippet: PDH deficiency caused by a Leu216Ser mutation in PDHA1.
